games.explanation: Kirby's name still carries reputation weight the underlying numbers stopped supporting a while ago, and the market keeps pricing him like it's 2023. Righties in particular are squaring him up at a .321 xwOBA clip, which is a problem when Stowers is the exact profile built to punish him. Meyer isn't the story here, but he doesn't need to be against a Seattle lineup that runs cold stretches like clockwork indoors. Getting the better team on the Elo board at a plus-money-adjacent price is the whole ballgame, and -116 undersells it by roughly seven points of win probability.

Toronto is the shinier name, but a pick-em price on a road favorite in a pitcher's park is the kind of small mistake the market makes when it's paying for logos. San Francisco actually grades out as the better side here, and Rafael Devers gets the platoon look he's been feasting on all year, with a .391 xwOBA against righties that plays anywhere in the lineup. McDonald is a small sample, but the whiff and chase numbers suggest the stuff travels, and the park quietly strips runs and home runs out of the box score before the lineups even take BP. Getting a live dog price on the better projected team is the whole reason to click this one.

picks.explanation: Skubal is the best pitcher on the planet and the market has priced this like Detroit already won, which is exactly the setup where a runline number starts to look generous. The sim sees a coin flip on the scoreboard, largely because Ginn is competent enough to keep a Tigers lineup missing its usual thump from running away, and because Skubal against this A's order still means a handful of loud contact from Kurtz and Langeliers. Getting a buck and a half at -135 in a game the model projects as a 5-5 fight is the entire argument. You are not betting the A's to win, you are betting Skubal to pitch a normal Skubal game and still only win by one.

Skenes is the best pitcher on the planet and he's still only one man, which matters when the price on the other side is asking you to believe Pittsburgh will win this by multiple runs. That's the part the market keeps forgetting: the Pirates don't score, and a Skenes start typically ends with a tidy little 3-2 game where the bullpen gets a chance to spoil things. Atlanta runs out a left-heavy top of the order that Skenes has been merely very good against instead of untouchable, and Olson in particular is the kind of bat that can flip a close game with one swing. The sim lands on a projected margin under a run, which is exactly the shape of game where the runline prints even in a loss. Paying -150 for a coin flip with a cushion is the whole idea.

Kirby's name still carries reputation weight the underlying numbers stopped supporting a while ago, and the market keeps pricing him like it's 2023. Righties in particular are squaring him up at a .321 xwOBA clip, which is a problem when Stowers is the exact profile built to punish him. Meyer isn't the story here, but he doesn't need to be against a Seattle lineup that runs cold stretches like clockwork indoors. Getting the better team on the Elo board at a plus-money-adjacent price is the whole ballgame, and -116 undersells it by roughly seven points of win probability.

Wheeler is going to do Wheeler things, but a run and a half is a lot of cushion when the projected margin is a field goal in a bandbox. The Reds don't need to win this game, they need to lose it close, and Elly De La Cruz plus Sal Stewart against right-handed pitching is exactly the kind of top-of-order thump that keeps a game within one swing. Abbott isn't Wheeler, but he's competent enough to keep Schwarber from turning this into a laugher, and the park plays the same for both dugouts. When the sim spits out a three-tenths-of-a-run gap and the market prices it like a two-run favorite, you take the points and let the coin flip breathe.

Toronto is the shinier name, but a pick-em price on a road favorite in a pitcher's park is the kind of small mistake the market makes when it's paying for logos. San Francisco actually grades out as the better side here, and Rafael Devers gets the platoon look he's been feasting on all year, with a .391 xwOBA against righties that plays anywhere in the lineup. McDonald is a small sample, but the whiff and chase numbers suggest the stuff travels, and the park quietly strips runs and home runs out of the box score before the lineups even take BP. Getting a live dog price on the better projected team is the whole reason to click this one.
